title: How China Is Handling AI’s Risk To Jobs
gist: China's government is embracing the AI revolution and, unlike the US, is not panicking over or policy-planning for AI-driven job destruction. Gavekal argues this is analytically justified because predicted AI labor-market disruption in the US has so far failed to show up in the data.

insights.claim: China's government is all-in on the AI revolution, with Xi Jinping framing AI as a new engine of economic growth, so US-style doom-mongering about AI's impact on employment has had little influence on Chinese official rhetoric or policy.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Chinese AI policy will keep prioritizing deployment and growth over labor protection, a tailwind for Chinese AI-adoption and automation plays rather than a regulatory brake.
insights.quote: AI is “a new engine of world economic growth and an accelerator of the transition from old to new growth drivers.”

insights.claim: China's government sees no need to panic over AI and is not preparing for scenarios in which AI wipes out half of entry-level white-collar jobs or pushes unemployment above 10%.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Expect little Chinese fiscal or safety-net response to AI job risk, so any labor shock there would hit an unprepared policy backdrop.
insights.quote: The government sees no need to panic, and is not preparing for scenarios in which AI wipes out half of entry-level white-collar jobs or pushes unemployment above 10%.
insights.locator: The government sees no need to panic

themes.theme: US AI labor-market disruption not materializing

insights.claim: Despite large language models spreading faster and wider in the US than in China, the predicted US labor-market disruption from AI has so far failed to materialize.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The bear case that AI is imminently gutting employment lacks data support, undercutting near-term macro fears tied to AI-driven unemployment.
insights.quote: the predicted labor-market disruption in the US, where use of large language models has spread faster and wider than in China, has so far failed to materialize
insights.locator: Analytically, that has been the correct stance

insights.claim: Most research finds no aggregate impact of AI on US labor-market trends, though there is specific concern about demand for entry-level workers and software engineers.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Watch entry-level hiring and software-engineer demand as the leading indicators where any AI displacement would show up first.
insights.quote: Most research finds no aggregate impact of AI on US labor-market trends, though there is concern about demand for entry-level workers and software engineers.
insights.locator: Most research finds no aggregate impact

insights.claim: Labor-market stability under AI may persist because firms haven't yet learned to replace workers with AI, because AI complements rather than replaces skilled workers, or because AI-driven productivity raises demand for workers' output.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: If AI is a complement that lifts output demand, the productivity-boom bull case holds; if it is merely early, displacement risk is deferred not cancelled.
insights.quote: AI in practice is more of a complement to than a replacement for skilled workers, or because higher productivity from AI ends up raising demand for workers’ output
insights.locator: The stability could be because companies
